title: “SBC”, decoded — and how a term handover actually works
summary: Two things every SIS builder hears and half-understands: the acronym “SBC”, and the term “handover” / rollover at the end of a semester. Today we pin down what SBC honestly means in an education context, then walk the real mechanics of rolling one term into the next.

activities.title: First, an honest answer about “SBC”
activities.markdown:
Here's the truth a good engineer should internalize: **“SBC” is not a single standardized acronym in education.** Authoritative sources don't define one canonical meaning, so beware of anyone who states it as fact.

In an SIS / curriculum context, the most relevant expansion is **Standards-Based Curriculum** (closely tied to **Standards-Based Grading**): teaching and assessment built around predetermined state/national **learning standards**, with aligned assessments.

⚠️ Watch out: the same three letters appear elsewhere with unrelated meanings (e.g., a screen literally named *SBC* in some Colleague SIS registrar workflows). When a term is ambiguous, **say so and cite** — don't guess. That habit is exactly what keeps a knowledge base free of false positives.

sources.title: Standards-Based — The Glossary of Education Reform

activities.question: In an education/SIS context, “SBC” most plausibly maps to which idea — keeping in mind it isn't a single standardized acronym?
activities.explanation: There's no authoritative single definition of “SBC” in education, but the most relevant mapping for an SIS/curriculum context is Standards-Based Curriculum (and the related Standards-Based Grading). The honest move is to flag the ambiguity rather than assert one expansion as fact.

sources.title: Standards-Based — The Glossary of Education Reform

activities.title: Standards-based, briefly
activities.markdown:
**Standards-based** approaches anchor curriculum and grading to **explicit learning standards** rather than to seat time or point accumulation.

- **Standards-Based Curriculum:** what's taught is mapped to predetermined standards, with assessments aligned to those standards.
- **Standards-Based Grading:** a grade reports a student's **mastery of specific standards**, instead of averaging miscellaneous points (homework, extra credit, behavior) into one number.

For an SIS, this matters because the **gradebook data model** must be able to attach results to *standards*, not just to assignments — which is also exactly why OneRoster 1.2 added richer score scales.

sources.title: Standards-Based — The Glossary of Education Reform

activities.question: Standards-Based Grading primarily measures…
options: a student's mastery of specific learning standards
how many points were accumulated, regardless of topic
activities.explanation: Standards-based grading reports mastery of specific learning standards, rather than blending unrelated points into a single average.

sources.title: Standards-Based — The Glossary of Education Reform

activities.title: Term handover = “term rollover”
activities.markdown:
When one term ends and the next begins, an SIS performs a **term rollover** (a.k.a. term roll / term setup). The core mechanic:

1. The registrar **creates the new term** and academic calendar dates.
2. The system **copies the prior *like-term*'s schedule of classes forward** as a template — fall→fall, spring→spring — because annual offerings repeat.
3. **Not everything copies.** Things like **instructor assignments, class notes, and canceled sections** are typically dropped and must be re-entered.
4. Departments **edit sections** until a **schedule-restriction date**, after which only limited fields (instructor, waitlist, etc.) stay editable.
5. The schedule is **published**, **registration windows open**, and the **academic calendar** governs term dates.

sources.title: Class Scheduling Workflow — Penn State Registrar

activities.title: The term rollover flow
activities.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  A[Prior like-term schedule] -->|roll forward as template| B[New term: draft sections]
  R[Registrar creates new term + calendar] --> B
  B --> C{Department edits\nbefore restriction date}
  C -->|drop: instructors, notes, cancelled sections| C
  C --> D[Schedule published]
  D --> E[Registration windows open]
  E --> F[Term active]

steps.title: Prior like-term schedule
steps.caption: Start from last fall's schedule as the template.

steps.title: Registrar creates the new term
steps.caption: Set up the new term and academic-calendar dates.

steps.title: Draft sections
steps.caption: The like-term schedule rolls forward into draft sections.

steps.title: Departments edit
steps.caption: Edits allowed until the schedule-restriction date; instructors, notes, and cancelled sections are dropped.

steps.title: Schedule published
steps.caption: The finalized schedule of classes goes live.

activities.caption: Roll the like term forward, edit before the restriction date, publish, open registration. The like-term mapping (fall→fall) preserves seasonal course patterns.

sources.title: Class Scheduling Workflow — Penn State Registrar

activities.question: During a term rollover, which of these does NOT typically carry forward automatically?
activities.explanation: The like-term schedule rolls forward as a template, but instructor assignments, class notes, and cancelled sections are usually dropped and must be re-entered for the new term.

activities.front: Why roll a **like term** (fall→fall) instead of the immediately preceding term?
activities.back: Because academic offerings repeat **annually**: last fall's schedule is the best template for next fall. Rolling the previous *like* term preserves seasonal course patterns better than copying, say, spring into fall.

activities.headline: Terms are interoperable too: the new Ed-Fi OneRoster Service
activities.summary: Term/semester data isn't just internal — standards model it explicitly. OneRoster represents terms as **`academicSessions`**. In the **Spring 2026** release (May 11, 2026), the new **Ed-Fi OneRoster Service** can serve OneRoster v1.2 endpoints — including academic sessions — directly from Ed-Fi data, so the term structure you build during rollover can flow to partner systems in a standard shape.

items: **“SBC” has no single canonical meaning** — most relevant SIS mapping is **Standards-Based Curriculum/Grading**; flag ambiguity instead of guessing.
**Standards-based grading** ties grades to **mastery of standards**, which shapes how a gradebook data model must store results.
**Term rollover** copies the prior *like-term* schedule forward; **instructors, notes, and cancelled sections don't carry over**.
Registration opens only after departments edit and the schedule is published — a natural place for **approval gates** in an SIS workflow.

glossary.definition: Curriculum built around predetermined state/national learning standards, with assessments aligned to those standards. Note: “SBC” is not a single standardized acronym in education.
A grading approach that reports a student's mastery of specific learning standards rather than averaging unrelated points into one score.
The SIS process of setting up a new term by copying the prior like-term's schedule of classes forward as a template; instructor assignments, notes, and cancelled sections typically do not carry over.
A term, semester, or grading period. OneRoster models these explicitly as `academicSessions`.
The set of course sections offered in a term. The prior like-term's schedule is the template used during term rollover.

sources.title: Class Scheduling Workflow — Penn State Registrar

reflection: ManageArk is schema-per-tenant with UUID v7 IDs. Sketch a “term rollover” as a background job: what copies, what resets, and what must a registrar explicitly approve before registration opens?
